title: Nuno felting a silk scarf
subtitle: Wool felted into a silk base to create a crinkled, textured fabric
excerpt: Lay merino roving in thin patches over a silk scarf base, wet-felt the assembly, and the wool fibres migrate through the weave of the silk and lock in permanently, puckering the silk into a textured, crinkled fabric. Nuno felting produces a lightweight cloth with the drape of silk and the texture of felt.
sourceNotes: Nuno felting was developed and named by Sachiko Kotaka in the late 1980s in Japan. The technique itself is not patented and the method is observation-led. 'Nuno' is the Japanese word for cloth. All modern teaching of the technique is in copyright; this tutorial draws on the physical principles of how wool barbs migrate through an open-weave base fabric.

content.text: silk base migrates through the weave under hot water and agitation. As the fibres lock into the threads of the silk, they pull the silk into crinkles, producing a lightweight, textured cloth. The technique suits a scarf format because the piece can be rolled and rubbed efficiently on a bamboo mat.
of 30-40% is standard in the warp direction: a 90 cm length of silk produces a scarf about 55-60 cm long after felting.

attrs.body: The base fabric must be an open-weave natural fibre. Silk chiffon (open plain weave) and silk gauze are reliable. Silk habotai is too tightly woven. Cotton muslin works at a coarser scale but the handle is less refined than silk. Synthetic fabrics do not accept wool fibres.
attrs.title: Fabric choice

items.name: Silk chiffon or gauze, approximately 45 x 90 cm

items.qty: piece at least as large as the silk
items.name: Felting net or tulle
Bubble wrap (optional, as alternative rolling surface)

content.text: Step 1: Lay the silk and add the roving

Lay the bamboo mat flat. Place the pre-washed silk on the mat, smoothing out any creases. Pull thin wisps of roving and lay them across the silk surface. The roving should not be a solid layer: lay it as thin, sparse patches and streaks, covering about 40-60% of the silk surface. Thick layers felt slowly and can produce a stiff, heavy scarf; thin layers felt quickly and produce a lightweight, crinkled cloth with the silk visible between the wool patches. This is the main design decision in nuno felting.
Step 2: Cover with net and wet down

Lay the felting net over the silk and roving. Rub the soap bar across the net surface. Pour hot water over the net until the silk and roving are thoroughly wet. Press the net flat with your palms to flatten any standing wisps of roving against the silk surface. Work gently at this stage: the roving has no grip yet and aggressive rubbing will shift it out of position.
Step 3: Rub through the net for 5 minutes

Rub firmly through the net with flat palms. Work the whole length of the scarf in straight passes. After 5 minutes, lift a corner of the net and check: the roving patches should be beginning to felt into the surface and should not slip when you press them gently with a finger. If they still slide, continue rubbing through the net for another 2-3 minutes before removing it.
Step 4: Roll in the mat and full

Remove the net. Roll the silk and roving up inside the bamboo mat, jellyroll-style, with the roving face outward. Roll the mat backwards and forwards firmly for 2-3 minutes. Unroll, turn the scarf 90 degrees, re-roll, and repeat. This bilateral rolling causes the wool fibres to migrate through the silk weave from both the surface and the roll-compression sides simultaneously. The silk should begin to crinkle visibly after the first two rolling sessions.
Step 5: Shock with cold water and finish

After 20-30 minutes of rolling, plunge the scarf into a bowl of cold water. The thermal shock tightens the wool fibres and gives a final boost to the crinkle. Squeeze out the water, press the scarf flat between dry towels, and dry flat. The crinkling is permanent and survives gentle hand-washing in cool water.

attrs.intro: Three problems specific to nuno felting.

items.fix: Keep the net in place throughout the early rubbing stage. Pour water gently rather than flooding the layout. The net holds the roving in position until the fibres start to grip the silk.
items.cause: The water was added too fast, or the net was removed before the fibres had initial grip.
items.symptom: Roving patches shift during the initial wetting stage

items.fix: Nuno felting needs sufficient shrinkage to pucker the silk. If the wool has felted but the silk is still relatively flat, continue rolling in the mat for another 10-15 minutes with fresh hot water and soap.
items.cause: Roving coverage was too heavy, or the felting was insufficient to pull the silk threads.
items.symptom: Very little crinkle in the finished scarf

items.fix: Use a more open-weave silk (chiffon rather than habotai) and thinner layers of roving. The fibre must be able to physically pass through the gaps in the weave.
items.cause: The silk base has too tight a weave for the roving fibre diameter.
items.symptom: Roving has felted as a solid layer on top of the silk rather than migrating through it

attrs.heading: Common problems
